_comment: Scope is deliberately NARROW (decision 017, finding 3): we detect subprocess reach to a network binary, NOT in-process HTTP (requests/httpx). Detecting whether `requests.get(url)` is mocked needs interprocedural dataflow â€” an abstract interpreter, not a linter. Delegate that layer to pytest-socket et al; this adapter owns the layer nothing else covers (pytest-socket#401: subprocess settings 'are not carried over into the subprocess').

closure._comment: Follow repo-local imports only. A third-party import resolves to no file and is simply not followed â€” `import requests` is a signal, not a file to recurse into. THREE groups, and the resolver needs all three: (1) the module of a `from X import ...`, (2) its imported names â€” because `from pkg import mod` reaches pkg/mod.py, and the spawn lives in the NAME, not in pkg/__init__.py â€” and (3) a plain `import a.b` (which may list several: `import a.b, c.d`). The names group also accepts a parenthesized, multi-line list, which is what black and ruff produce. Leading dots on the module are LEVELS and the resolver honours them: `from ..runner import go` is the parent package, not this one.

_stub_scan_comment: Deliberately ABSENT. Widening the stub search to imported helpers needs the same 'does it actually run?' analysis the bash adapter does (top-level code + functions the suite calls); without it, a stub merely DEFINED in a helper would exempt every module that imports it. Python's shape here is conftest fixtures, which is a different analysis. Until that exists, a Python stub must be visible in the test file itself â€” a false positive costs one line, a false pass costs an incident.

stub_rules._comment: The spawn mechanism itself is patched. The target must BE subprocess (or its API) â€” an earlier version matched any patch() whose arguments merely contained the substring `subprocess`, so `mock.patch("app.subprocess_helper.run")` marked the whole file stubbed for every binary.

PATH shim â€” the direct analogue of the bash pattern, and like it, TWO facts must co-occur: a file NAMED AFTER THE BINARY is created, AND PATH is pointed at the dir holding it. Accepting the PATH half alone was a false pass: the old rule `os.environ["PATH"]` matched a bare *read* â€” `env = dict(PATH=os.environ["PATH"])`, the ordinary way to build a subprocess env â€” and marked every binary in the file stubbed, greenlighting a suite that really did spawn curl.

_optout_comment: `pytestmark` is genuinely file-level, so honouring it file-wide is honest. A bare `@pytest.mark.network` decorator is NOT: it marks one test, but opted_out() is file-scoped, so one legitimately-networked integration test at the top of a module silently exempted every other test in the file â€” including ones added later that shell out unstubbed. That is precisely 014's failure mode (a silent escape hatch), so the decorator is not accepted; use `pytestmark` or the `@network: allowed` comment, both of which mean what the lint reads them to mean.
